title: Scaling LLM Test-Time Compute Optimally Can be More Effective than Scaling Parameters for Reasoning
claim: Difficulty-adaptive compute-optimal test-time scaling improves efficiency >4x over best-of-N; in a separate FLOPs-matched comparison, a smaller model with test-time compute outperforms a 14x larger model on prompts where the base model has non-trivial success rate. Envelope: PaLM-2-class models on MATH with fine-tuned revision models and PRMs; difficulty estimation itself costs test-time compute; the two findings are separate comparisons, not one exchange rate; generalisation beyond math/easily-verified domains left unresolved by the authors; 14x depends on difficulty and assumed inference-to-pretraining ratio.

title: Inference Scaling Laws: An Empirical Analysis of Compute-Optimal Inference for Problem-Solving with Language Models
claim: Llemma-7B with tree search consistently outperforms Llemma-34B on MATH at equal compute; smaller models + advanced inference algorithms are Pareto-optimal in cost-performance.

title: Training Verifiers to Solve Math Word Problems
claim: Trained outcome verifiers + sample-and-rank significantly improve GSM8K and verification scales more effectively with data than finetuning. NOTE: the folk-cited '6B+verifier matches 30x larger finetuned model' figure was NOT verified at abstract level.

title: Let's Verify Step by Step
claim: Process supervision significantly outperforms outcome supervision on MATH; PRM-guided selection solves 78% of a representative MATH subset; PRM800K = 800k step-level human labels released.

title: Math-Shepherd: Verify and Reinforce LLMs Step-by-step without Human Annotations
claim: Automatically constructed process supervision (no human labels) trains a PRM; Mistral-7B improves 77.9->84.1 GSM8K via step-PPO, 89.1 GSM8K / 43.5 MATH with verification.

title: Small Language Models Need Strong Verifiers to Self-Correct Reasoning
claim: Models <=13B cannot reliably self-correct reasoning; improvement requires a strong external verifier; weak self-verification is the binding constraint.

title: Scaling Flaws of Verifier-Guided Search in Mathematical Reasoning
claim: As sample budget grows, verifier-guided search exhibits diminishing advantages and eventually underperforms repeated sampling (Mistral-7B, DeepSeekMath-7B; GSM8K/MATH; outcome and process verifiers); mechanism: imperfect verifiers misrank and erroneously prune all valid paths, worst on hard/OOD problems.

title: Generalization through Memorization: Nearest Neighbor Language Models
claim: Interpolating a strong Wikitext-103 LM with a kNN datastore over the training set achieves SOTA perplexity 15.79 (-2.9) with no additional training; cost is datastore + per-token ANN search, uncharged in the headline.

title: Improving language models by retrieving from trillions of tokens
claim: RETRO with a 2-trillion-token retrieval database achieves performance comparable to GPT-3/Jurassic-1 with 25x fewer parameters; RETROfit adds retrieval to pretrained transformers. Parameter claim excludes database bytes.

title: Atlas: Few-shot Learning with Retrieval Augmented Language Models
claim: Retrieval-augmented 11B model reaches >42% on 64-shot NQ, outperforming a 540B model by 3% with 50x fewer parameters (index bytes excluded from the size claim).

title: When Not to Trust Language Models: Investigating Effectiveness of Parametric and Non-Parametric Memories
claim: On PopQA (14k questions, 10 models), retrieval-augmented LMs largely outperform orders-of-magnitude larger LMs on long-tail entities; scaling fails to improve long-tail memorization; adaptive (popularity-thresholded) retrieval improves accuracy and cuts inference cost.

title: Scaling Retrieval-Based Language Models with a Trillion-Token Datastore
claim: Datastore scaling (up to 1.4T tokens, MassiveDS) monotonically improves LM and downstream performance without obvious saturation; a smaller model with a large datastore outperforms a larger LM-only model on knowledge-intensive tasks; compute-optimal curves favor larger datastores at fixed training compute.

title: Self-RAG: Learning to Retrieve, Generate, and Critique through Self-Reflection
claim: 7B/13B LMs trained with reflection tokens (learned adaptive retrieval + self-critique) outperform ChatGPT and retrieval-augmented Llama2-chat on open-domain QA, reasoning and fact verification.

title: Toolformer: Language Models Can Teach Themselves to Use Tools
claim: Self-supervised API-call insertion (calculator, QA, search, translation, calendar) yields substantially improved zero-shot performance often competitive with much larger models.

title: APIGen: Automated Pipeline for Generating Verifiable and Diverse Function-Calling Datasets
claim: 60k execution-verified synthetic function-calling examples spanning 3,673 executable APIs across 21 categories; the 1B model trained on them surpasses GPT-3.5-Turbo and Claude-3 Haiku on the Berkeley Function-Calling Benchmark; the 7B model outperforms multiple GPT-4 variants. Function-calling-specific but broad within that scope - not narrow-curated in the TinyAgent sense.

title: TinyAgent: Function Calling at the Edge
claim: TinyAgent-1.1B fine-tuned on 40k curated use-cases with tool retrieval (ToolRAG) + quantisation reports 80.06% success vs GPT-4-Turbo 79.08% on its own curated task suite - a 16-tool, task-specific Mac-assistant benchmark (domain-narrow by construction). Not resource-matched: ToolRAG includes a separately fine-tuned DeBERTa tool selector whose weights, training and inference belong in the system ledger.

title: From Local to Global: A Graph RAG Approach to Query-Focused Summarization
claim: LLM-built entity graph + community summaries substantially improves comprehensiveness/diversity over conventional RAG on global sensemaking questions over ~1M-token corpora; index built by LLM passes. The paper reports construction/runtime information including indexing wall time for its corpora, but no complete bytes/build-compute lifecycle ledger.

title: HippoRAG: Neurobiologically Inspired Long-Term Memory for Large Language Models
claim: KG + Personalized PageRank single-step retrieval matches or beats iterative retrieval (IRCoT) on multi-hop QA while 10-30x cheaper and 6-13x faster online; outperforms SOTA by up to 20% on multi-hop benchmarks; graph is LLM-extracted offline.

title: RAG vs. GraphRAG: A Systematic Evaluation and Key Insights
claim: Under a unified protocol (same preprocessing, retrieval config, generation settings), neither RAG nor GraphRAG uniformly dominates; strengths are task-dependent and combining them yields consistent improvements.

title: When to use Graphs in RAG: A Comprehensive Analysis for Graph Retrieval-Augmented Generation
claim: GraphRAG frequently underperforms vanilla RAG on many real-world tasks; introduces GraphRAG-Bench with difficulty-graded tasks and stage-wise evaluation (construction -> retrieval -> generation) to locate where graph structure pays.

title: LightRAG: Simple and Fast Retrieval-Augmented Generation
claim: Dual-level (entity/relation graph + vector) retrieval with incremental index updates; claims considerable improvements in retrieval accuracy and efficiency over existing approaches. Quantitative cost tables NOT verified at abstract level.

title: MiniRAG: Towards Extremely Simple Retrieval-Augmented Generation
claim: Heterogeneous graph index (chunks + named entities) + topology-enhanced retrieval lets small LMs achieve performance comparable to LLM-based RAG while requiring only 25% of the storage (author-reported relative comparison vs their own LLM-RAG baseline, not a full deployment-budget measurement). The reading that graph structure 'substitutes for' weak SLM embedding semantics is the authors' causal interpretation, stronger than the reported system comparison.

title: KILT: a Benchmark for Knowledge Intensive Language Tasks
claim: Grounds all knowledge-intensive tasks in the same pinned Wikipedia snapshot and evaluates provenance jointly with answers - the precedent for pinning the source snapshot and scoring provenance. It does NOT mandate identical retrievers across systems and is no precedent for cross-representation (kernel records vs passages vs triples vs tool schemas) matched controls.

title: BEIR: A Heterogenous Benchmark for Zero-shot Evaluation of Information Retrieval Models
claim: Across 18 datasets, BM25 is a robust zero-shot baseline that dense retrievers frequently fail to beat out-of-domain; reranking/late-interaction models win zero-shot but are computationally expensive.

title: Leveraging Passage Retrieval with Generative Models for Open Domain Question Answering
claim: Fusion-in-Decoder: open-domain QA accuracy significantly improves as the number of retrieved passages aggregated by the generative reader increases - generation-side evidence aggregation, not retrieval precision alone, drove SOTA; retrieval and generation quality must be reported separately.

title: Lost in the Middle: How Language Models Use Long Contexts
claim: Performance is highest when relevant information occurs at the beginning or end of the input context and significantly degrades mid-context - context position is a hidden variable in any RAG comparison.

title: The Power of Noise: Redefining Retrieval for RAG Systems
claim: High-scoring but irrelevant retrieved documents hurt accuracy, while adding random documents improved LLM accuracy by up to 35% in their setting - a measured RAG 'win' can be a prompt-perturbation effect; mandates a random-document control arm.

title: FlashRAG: A Modular Toolkit for Efficient Retrieval-Augmented Generation Research
claim: 16 reproduced RAG methods + 38 standardized benchmark datasets per the arXiv v2 abstract (revised 2025-02-24, re-fetched 2026-07-11; the 2024 v1 abstract reported 12 methods + 32 datasets - pin the release actually used), motivated by the absence of a standardized framework making RAG comparisons unreliable - implementation variance is a first-order confound.
